content.text: Why PRD Reviews for GenAI Features Need Multiple Gates

A standard software PRD typically goes through one review cycle: the product owner and engineering lead confirm the scope and acceptance criteria, and work begins. A GenAI feature PRD requires a structured multi-gate review because the failure consequences are distributed across more disciplines and are harder to catch after implementation begins. An undiscovered ambiguity in a traditional PRD becomes a misbuilt screen. An undiscovered ambiguity in a GenAI PRD becomes a safety risk, a legal exposure, or a fundamental architecture mismatch that requires re-engineering the agent's prompt, eval suite, and monitoring stack simultaneously.

The four-gate review structure described here is designed to separate concerns: completeness is checked before technical depth, technical depth is checked before safety, and safety is checked before business stakeholders invest sign-off time. This sequencing means that rejection at an early gate is cheap (the author has not yet spent time on more detailed sections), and rejection at a late gate is rare (early gates have already cleared the prerequisites).

attrs.alt: PRD Review and Approval Gate Flow
attrs.title: PRD Review Gates

content.text: Gate 1: Completeness Review

The completeness review is a checklist review that verifies every section of the PRD has been filled in. It does not evaluate the quality of the content — that is gate 2 and 3's job. Its sole purpose is to reject PRDs that have placeholder text, empty sections, or obviously missing elements before time is spent on more detailed review. A completeness gate prevents the waste of a deep technical review session that ends with "this section is still a placeholder — come back when it's done."

The completeness reviewer is typically the product manager who authored the PRD or a designated PRD owner on the team. They run through the sixteen-section checklist and mark each section as complete, incomplete, or deferred-with-justification. A deferred section is one that cannot be completed until a dependency is resolved (for example, the model selection in section 13 may depend on a vendor evaluation still in progress). Deferrals are acceptable at gate 1 only if they are time-bounded and do not block gate 2 or gate 3 items.
All 16 sections must be present and contain substantive content (not placeholder text).
Section 1 (job statement) must be in job-story format with a trigger, action, and outcome.
Section 3 (success metrics) must include baseline, target, and measurement method for each metric.
Section 4 (scope) must include both an in-scope list and an out-of-scope list.
Sections 9 and 10 must be populated, not cross-referenced to "TBD" documents.
Section 15 (launch gate criteria) must list specific, verifiable criteria — not generic statements like "feature is ready."
All deferred sections must be flagged with a specific resolution date and owner.
Gate 2: Technical Review

The technical review evaluates whether the PRD's behaviour specifications, prompt requirements, tool definitions, and infrastructure constraints are implementable and internally consistent. Participants are the conversation designer, the ML or AI engineer, and the backend engineer responsible for tool integration. The product owner attends to clarify intent but does not unilaterally overrule technical concerns — if an engineer says a behaviour requirement is impossible or prohibitively expensive, the product owner must either accept the constraint or revise the scope.
The technical review evaluates six categories:

Implementability of prompt requirements: can the model reliably follow every MUST and MUST NOT instruction? Instructions that the model's tendencies make difficult to enforce must be flagged, and alternative implementations (classifiers, UI affordances, retrieval filters) must be specified in the PRD before gate 2 passes.

Tool schema completeness: does every capability in the capability map (section 5) have a corresponding tool definition (section 8)? Are the input and output schemas sufficient for the agent to extract the information it needs without additional tool calls?

Latency budget feasibility: given the sequence of tool calls the agent must make to resolve the primary job, is the p95 latency target in section 13 achievable? If a single tool call takes 2 seconds at p95 and the journey requires three sequential tool calls, a total p95 latency target of 3 seconds is not achievable.

Context window budget: given the system prompt length (estimated from section 7), the expected conversation history, and the tool response sizes, does the interaction fit within the model's context window? If not, the PRD must specify a context management strategy.

Eval scenario coverage: does the sample set in section 9 include test cases for every category of prompt requirement? Technical reviewers check that each MUST, MUST NOT, and IF/THEN instruction has at least one corresponding eval scenario.

Consistency of sections 5, 7, and 8: the capability map, prompt requirements, and tool definitions must be consistent. If section 5 lists a capability that section 8 has no tool to support, or section 7 has instructions that reference a capability not in section 5, these inconsistencies must be resolved before gate 2 passes.
Gate 2 Approval Criteria and Output

GATE 2 TECHNICAL REVIEW CHECKLIST

Prompt Requirements (Section 7)
  [ ] All MUST instructions are implementable in a system prompt (no known model limitations)
  [ ] All MUST NOT instructions have a paired alternative behaviour
  [ ] All IF/THEN conditions are unambiguous and testable
  [ ] No contradictory instructions exist in the document
  [ ] Prompt requirements include tool use instructions covering timeout, retry, and fallback
  [ ] Safety constraint phrasings reviewed for clarity and coverage

Tool Definitions (Section 8)
  [ ] Every capability in section 5 has a corresponding tool entry
  [ ] All tool input schemas are sufficient (no missing required fields)
  [ ] All tool output schemas include error schema with typed error codes
  [ ] Timeout budget specified for each tool
  [ ] Fallback behaviour specified for each error type
  [ ] Access control requirements stated (who can invoke, what auth is required)

Infrastructure (Section 13)
  [ ] Latency targets are achievable given tool call chain depth
  [ ] Context window budget has been estimated and margin is > 20%
  [ ] Cost ceiling has been calculated against expected usage volume
  [ ] Model version policy is specified (pinned or auto-update with eval gate)

Eval Coverage (Section 9)
  [ ] At least one eval scenario per MUST instruction
  [ ] At least one adversarial eval scenario per MUST NOT instruction
  [ ] At least one scenario pair per IF/THEN conditional
  [ ] At least one multi-turn scenario per context retention requirement
  [ ] Failure mode scenarios cover all FM-register items at Functional severity+

Gate 2 Outcome:
  PASS: all items checked, signed off by AI engineer and conversation designer
  CONDITIONAL PASS: minor items noted for resolution before sprint 1 planning
  REJECT: blocker items present; PRD returned for revision
Gate 3: Safety and Ethics Review

The safety and ethics review evaluates sections 10 (failure mode register), 11 (safety and ethics), and 12 (data and privacy). The reviewer is a designated AI safety reviewer — typically a senior engineer or product manager with specific training in AI risk assessment — accompanied by legal or compliance representation for features in regulated domains.

Gate 3 has four specific concerns that differ from the technical review. First, severity completeness: are all failure modes of Trust-destroying severity or above mitigated at Tier 2 or higher? A failure mode register that shows a Trust-destroying entry with only a Tier 1 (prompt guardrail) mitigation is a gate 3 blocker. Second, safety constraint coverage: does section 11 cover the safety policy requirements for the feature's domain and user population? Missing prohibited content categories, inadequate escalation triggers, or no human escalation path are gate 3 blockers. Third, data minimisation: does section 12 specify data retention and access control for every data type the agent accesses or stores? Data collected without a stated purpose and retention policy is both a GDPR violation risk and a gate 3 blocker. Fourth, bias and fairness: has the feature been reviewed for differential impact across user segments? For features that make recommendations or prioritise information, demographic bias in outputs is a gate 3 concern.
All FM-register items of Trust-destroying or above severity must have Tier 2+ mitigations approved by the safety reviewer.
Section 11 must cover all safety policy categories relevant to the domain (prohibited content, crisis handling, regulated advice restrictions).
Section 12 must specify retention policy and access control for every data type. No data types with "TBD" retention are acceptable at gate 3.
Features in regulated domains (financial, medical, legal, children's products) require explicit compliance sign-off from the relevant legal or compliance team member before gate 3 passes.
The launch gate checklist in section 15 must reference the safety review sign-off date as a required pre-launch item.
Gate 4: Stakeholder Sign-off

Gate 4 is the business and product stakeholder review. By the time the PRD reaches gate 4, it has already passed technical and safety reviews, so stakeholders can focus on business alignment rather than implementation correctness. The gate 4 audience includes the product owner, the business sponsor, and any cross-functional stakeholders who are impacted by the feature (customer success, legal, marketing, compliance).

Gate 4 evaluates three things: alignment with the product strategy (does this feature serve the right user jobs and business goals?), resource commitment (are the sprint capacity estimates and delivery timeline realistic?), and risk acceptance (are the accepted risks in section 16 acceptable given the business context?). Gate 4 should not be used to re-open technical or safety questions — those were resolved in gates 2 and 3. If a stakeholder raises a new technical or safety concern at gate 4, the review is paused and the concern is routed back to the appropriate gate.
Gate 4 Sign-off Artefact

PRD SIGN-OFF RECORD — [Feature Name] v[version]

Gate 1 — Completeness
  Reviewer: [Product Manager name]
  Date: [date]
  Status: APPROVED
  Notes: [any conditional items and resolution dates]

Gate 2 — Technical Review
  Reviewers: [AI Engineer name], [Conversation Designer name]
  Date: [date]
  Status: APPROVED
  Open items (if conditional pass): [list with owner and due date]

Gate 3 — Safety and Ethics
  Reviewer: [AI Safety Reviewer name]
  Compliance sign-off: [Legal/Compliance name] (if regulated domain)
  Date: [date]
  Status: APPROVED
  Open items: [none] or [list with owner and due date]

Gate 4 — Stakeholder Sign-off
  Product Owner: [name]  Signature: ___________  Date: [date]
  Business Sponsor: [name]  Signature: ___________  Date: [date]
  Legal/Compliance: [name]  Signature: ___________  Date: [date]
  Status: APPROVED

This sign-off authorises:
  [ ] Sprint 1 planning to begin
  [ ] Prompt registry entry to be created
  [ ] Eval suite repository to be initialised
  [ ] Tool API contracts to be drafted

Version history:
  v1.0 — [date] — Initial approved version
  v1.1 — [date] — [change description], approved by [reviewer]
Handling PRD Rejections Constructively

A PRD rejection at any gate should be treated as a design win, not a failure. The gate has done its job: it caught a gap, inconsistency, or risk before it became a sprint cost, an incident, or a compliance exposure. The most important cultural practice around PRD review gates is making rejection a neutral, routine event rather than a negative signal about the author's competence.

When a gate rejects a PRD, the feedback must be specific and actionable. "This section needs more detail" is not actionable. "Section 7 is missing MUST NOT instructions for the three out-of-scope categories listed in section 4" is actionable. The gate reviewer is responsible for documenting every rejection item with the section number, the specific gap, and (where possible) an example of what a passing version would look like.
The author revises only the items flagged in the rejection, not the entire PRD. Re-review is limited to the flagged items to avoid review fatigue.
Rejection at gate 2 or 3 does not reset the gate 1 approval — the completeness review does not need to be repeated for targeted revisions.
If the same item is flagged at re-review, the product manager and reviewer should meet directly to resolve the underlying disagreement rather than bouncing the PRD through another review cycle.

Track the number of gate iterations per PRD as a process health metric. PRDs that require more than two iterations at any gate indicate either a template problem (the guidance in the PRD template is unclear) or a readiness problem (PRDs are being submitted before the author is ready).
Adapting the Gate Process for Small Teams

Four formal gates with separate reviewer pools are appropriate for teams of 10 or more people working on production-grade features. Smaller teams or earlier-stage features may need a lighter-weight adaptation.

A two-gate adaptation for small teams: Gate A (combines completeness + technical) is an async review where the engineer and conversation designer each read the PRD and add inline comments, followed by a 45-minute sync to resolve open items. Gate B (combines safety + stakeholder) is a 30-minute meeting where the safety reviewer presents findings and the product owner and business sponsor ask questions. Both gates together take two hours of meeting time rather than two to three days of asynchronous review cycles.

The minimum non-negotiable set for any team size: at least one engineer must confirm implementability before work begins, at least one person designated as the safety reviewer must approve the failure mode register, and the product owner must explicitly sign off on the job statement, scope, and launch gate criteria. Everything else can be streamlined without losing the essential safety properties the gate process is designed to enforce.
Gate 3 Safety Review Checklist in Full

The safety review checklist is the most consequential of the four gates and deserves its own detailed treatment. Below is a comprehensive safety gate checklist that covers the full scope of concerns for enterprise agentic AI features. Teams should adapt this checklist to their domain — a consumer chatbot will have different populated fields than a clinical decision support agent — but none of the categories should be omitted without explicit justification.

GATE 3 SAFETY REVIEW CHECKLIST — GenAI Feature

== SECTION A: FAILURE MODE REGISTER REVIEW ==
  [ ] Register exists and is linked from PRD section 10
  [ ] All failure modes assigned a severity level (Cosmetic through Catastrophic)
  [ ] Zero open failure modes at Trust-destroying severity without Tier 2+ mitigation
  [ ] Zero open failure modes at Safety-critical severity without Tier 3+ mitigation
  [ ] Zero open failure modes at Catastrophic severity (all must be Tier 4 blocked)
  [ ] Each mitigation has been described with enough specificity to implement
  [ ] Each mitigation has a corresponding eval scenario in section 9

== SECTION B: SAFETY CONSTRAINT COVERAGE (Section 11) ==
Content safety:
  [ ] Prohibited content categories listed (hate speech, violence, self-harm, etc.)
  [ ] Out-of-scope domain refusal phrasings are safe (do not further distress user)
  [ ] Response templates for crisis detection reviewed by domain expert
  [ ] Crisis detection vocabulary list is comprehensive for target user population
Bias and fairness:
  [ ] Feature has been reviewed for differential quality across demographic groups
  [ ] If the feature makes recommendations: bias audit planned before or at launch
  [ ] If the feature serves a vulnerable population: additional review completed
AI transparency:
  [ ] Agent does not claim to be human when sincerely asked
  [ ] Agent does not claim capabilities it does not have
  [ ] Agent uncertainty is expressed when confidence is low
Regulatory:
  [ ] If financial advice domain: FCA / SEC / relevant regulator constraints addressed
  [ ] If medical domain: no diagnosis, prescription, or clinical decision-making
  [ ] If legal domain: explicit disclaimer that output is not legal advice
  [ ] If children's product: COPPA / GDPR-K compliance confirmed

== SECTION C: DATA AND PRIVACY (Section 12) ==
  [ ] Every data type the agent accesses is listed with source and access control
  [ ] Every data type the agent stores is listed with retention period and deletion policy
  [ ] Lawful basis for processing personal data is identified (GDPR or equivalent)
  [ ] Conversation logs: retention period confirmed with data governance team
  [ ] PII handling: agent must not log PII unless consent and legal basis are confirmed
  [ ] Cross-border data transfer: confirmed if applicable
  [ ] Data subject rights: process for handling deletion and access requests confirmed

== SECTION D: HUMAN ESCALATION PATH ==
  [ ] At least one human escalation path defined for each failure mode class
  [ ] Escalation contact (team, queue, or individual) is confirmed and available
  [ ] Escalation SLA is specified (e.g., response within 4 business hours)
  [ ] Context transfer on escalation: conversation history passed to human agent
  [ ] Escalation path has been tested in staging with a complete end-to-end scenario

== SECTION E: LAUNCH GATE CRITERIA REVIEW (Section 15) ==
  [ ] All safety review items referenced in launch gate checklist
  [ ] Safety reviewer named as required sign-off in launch gate
  [ ] Post-launch safety monitoring plan exists (metric, owner, escalation threshold)
  [ ] Incident response plan updated to include this feature's failure scenarios

Gate 3 Outcome:
  APPROVED — All items checked, safety reviewer signs off
  CONDITIONAL APPROVED — Minor items with resolution plan and date
  REJECTED — Blockers present; PRD returned for mitigation work
Running the Review as an Asynchronous Process

The four-gate process does not require four synchronous meetings. Gates 1 and 2 can be run asynchronously: the PRD is submitted to a shared review document or pull request, reviewers add comments with the section number and the specific gap, and the author resolves comments asynchronously. A brief sync meeting is only required if a comment thread does not converge after two rounds of written exchanges.

Gate 3 (safety) works best as a synchronous review because safety concerns often require nuanced discussion that written comments do not resolve efficiently. The safety reviewer reads the PRD async in advance, prepares a list of concerns, and the sync meeting is used to discuss and resolve them with the product owner and AI engineer present. Gate 4 (stakeholder sign-off) is always synchronous — it is an alignment meeting, not just a checklist review.
Use a shared document platform (Notion, Confluence, Google Docs) with comment threads for asynchronous gates. Each comment thread should have an assignee and a due date.
Use a pull request workflow for teams that store PRDs in the repository. PR reviewers can be assigned per gate. Gate 1 reviewer approves, then gate 2 reviewers are added, and so on.
Track gate status in the project management tool (Linear, Jira) as a PRD review epic with four child tasks. The sprint planning meeting is blocked on all four tasks being in Done status.

Set a service-level agreement for review turnaround: gate 1 within 1 business day, gate 2 within 3 business days, gate 3 within 2 business days, gate 4 within 2 business days. An SLA encourages reviewers to prioritise PRD review rather than deprioritise it in favour of other work.
Integrating the Gate Process With Existing Delivery Workflows

Teams that use Scrum, Kanban, or Shape Up need to integrate the four-gate review into their existing delivery workflow without creating a separate, disconnected process. The most common failure mode is treating the PRD gate review as a waterfall phase that happens before any agile work begins — this creates a bottleneck and erodes team confidence in the process. Instead, the gate process should be concurrent with early-stage discovery work such as technical spikes, vendor evaluations, and prototype experiments.

A recommended integration for Scrum teams: run PRD authoring and gate reviews during the sprint in which the product owner completes discovery. The sprint delivers two things: (1) a completed, signed-off PRD and (2) a technical spike that validates the most uncertain technical assumptions in the PRD. At the end of the sprint, the team has both the specification and the evidence that the specification is feasible. Sprint 1 (implementation) begins immediately in the next sprint. This structure keeps the gate reviews from being a separate waterfall phase while ensuring the specification quality that gates provide.

For teams using Shape Up, the gate process maps naturally to the shaping phase. The bet table review at the end of shaping corresponds to gate 4. The technical and safety reviews happen during shaping, before the feature is betted. The pitch document becomes the PRD. Teams adapting the gate process to Shape Up should ensure that the pitch template includes the safety and ethics section (section 11) and the failure mode register (section 10), which are not part of the standard Shape Up pitch format.
Summary

The four-gate PRD review process — completeness, technical, safety, and stakeholder sign-off — is the mechanism that ensures a GenAI feature PRD is complete, implementable, safe, and business-aligned before sprint work begins. Each gate has specific reviewers, specific criteria, and specific sign-off artefacts. Rejections are treated as valuable quality signals, not failures, and are resolved with targeted revisions rather than full rewrites. For small teams, a two-gate adaptation preserves the essential safety properties at lower process overhead. In either form, the gate process is what transforms a PRD from a document that describes intent into a specification that authorises and guides production-quality engineering work.
